elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce the task of text-to-diagram generation, which focuses on creating structured visual representations directly from textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-002: Existing approaches in text-to-image and text-to-code generation lack the logical organization and flexibility needed to produce accurate, editable diagrams, often resulting in outputs that are either unstructured or difficult to modify.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this gap, we introduce DiagramGenBenchmark, a comprehensive evaluation framework encompassing eight distinct diagram categories, including flowcharts, model architecture diagrams, and mind maps.
elements.sent-2411-004: Additionally, we present DiagramAgent, an innovative framework with four core modules—Plan Agent, Code Agent, Check Agent, and Diagram-to-Code Agent—designed to facilitate both the generation and refinement of complex diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our extensive experiments, which combine objective metrics with human evaluations, demonstrate that DiagramAgent significantly outperforms existing baseline models in terms of accuracy, structural coherence, and modifiability.
elements.sent-2411-006: This work not only establishes a foundational benchmark for the text-to-diagram generation task but also introduces a powerful toolset to advance research and applications in this emerging area.
elements.sent-2411-007: The task of transforming textual descriptions into structured diagrams, referred to as text-to-diagram generation, is crucial for domains that rely on clear, logical representations of complex information~\cite{mccracken2001text,guo2024survey,cao2024survey .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Unlike natural scene images, structured diagrams—such as flowcharts, mind maps, and model architecture diagrams—require precise relationships and logical coherence to effectively communicate intricate ideas.
elements.sent-2411-009: This study focuses on developing robust methods for automatic diagram generation and editing, aiming to facilitate efficient, accurate diagram creation from textual input.
elements.sent-2411-010: Such advancements hold great potential to enhance productivity in domains like education, scientific research, and industry, where structured visualizations are essential for effective communication and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-011: While recent advancements in text-to-image generation have produced impressive results in synthesizing photorealistic and aesthetically pleasing images~\cite{zhou2023vision+, cao2024survey , these methods are largely optimized for naturalistic visuals rather than structured, logical content.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Models such as GANs~\cite{stackgan, attngan and diffusion models~\cite{photorealistic, precisecontrol have excelled at generating images with rich visual detail, but they inherently lack the ability to enforce the precise relationships and hierarchical organization required for structured diagrams.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Alternative approaches have sought to first convert text into code representations~\cite{allamanis2018survey,kumar2023comprehensive , which are then compiled into charts or simple plots.}
elements.sent-2411-014: These methods leverage advancements in large language models to interpret textual descriptions as code that can produce visual output.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, while effective for generating basic visualizations such as bar charts and line graphs, this approach has limitations when applied to more complex diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-016: Specifically, they lack the flexibility and structural logic required for diagrams that demand intricate organization and interactivity to edits—a necessity for broader scientific and industrial applications.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{img/img1.pdf} \caption{Challenges in Existing Text-to-Image and Text-to-Code Methods for Diagram Generation} \label{fig:challenges}
elements.sent-2411-017: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:challenges , existing methods fall short when faced with tasks that require both logical precision and post-generation modifiability.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Text-to-image models often produce outputs that are visually appealing but logically incoherent~\cite{lin2025evaluating, zhou2023vision+ , failing to capture the structured relationships that intricate diagrams demand.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This gap in logical structure limits the practical utility of these models in scenarios where accurate and adaptable visual representations are essential.
elements.sent-2411-020: On the other hand, current text-to-code approaches, while suitable for simpler visualizations, struggle to represent complex diagram features such as hierarchical relationships, intricate color coding, and layered structures~\cite{allamanis2018survey, kumar2023comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Furthermore, there is a significant lack of comprehensive datasets tailored for generating and modifying diverse types of structured diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-022: Existing datasets tend to focus on either image realism or simplistic data visualizations, neither of which fully address the demands of text-to-diagram generation.
elements.sent-2411-023: To address these challenges, we introduce DiagramGenBenchmark, a rigorously curated dataset for evaluating structured diagram generation and editing.
elements.sent-2411-024: This benchmark includes eight distinct types of diagrams, such as flowcharts, bar charts, and model architectures, providing a solid foundation for advancing and assessing text-to-diagram tasks.
elements.sent-2411-025: Alongside this benchmark dataset, we propose DiagramAgent, a framework for text-to-diagram generation and editing.
elements.sent-2411-026: DiagramAgent integrates multiple agents, including a Plan Agent for interpreting textual commands, a Code Agent for generating diagram code, a Check Agent for logical verification, and a Diagram-to-Code Agent.
elements.sent-2411-027: This framework not only facilitates robust diagram generation but also enables editing and adaptation, accommodating complex structural requirements.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our extensive experiments on DiagramAgent, conducted using the DiagramGenBenchmark dataset, demonstrate its superior performance across various diagram types and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-029: Notably, DiagramAgent outperforms existing works in terms of accuracy and overall quality.
elements.sent-2411-030: For example, in the diagram generation task, DiagramAgent achieved top scores with Pass@1 reaching 58.15, ROUGE-L scoring 51.97, and CodeBLEU at 86.83, highlighting its efficacy in generating accurate and logically coherent diagram code.
elements.sent-2411-031: We further validated DiagramAgent's versatility by testing it on both open-source models, such as Qwen2~\cite{qwen2 , and closed-source models, including GPT-4o~\cite{gpt4 , demonstrating its adaptability across different model architectures.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: To substantiate these results, we conducted human evaluations that aligned closely with the objective metrics, confirming DiagramAgent's accuracy and usability in diagram generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-033: We summarize our contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-034: We introduce DiagramGenBenchmark, a benchmark dataset covering eight diverse types of diagrams, specifically tailored for diagram generation and editing.
elements.sent-2411-035: We present DiagramAgent, a text-to-diagram framework capable of both generating and editing diagrams, providing flexibility and accuracy in complex logical structures.
elements.sent-2411-036: We conduct comprehensive experiments demonstrating DiagramAgent's superior performance in terms of accuracy and logical coherence, establishing DiagramAgent as a reliable baseline for future research.
elements.sent-2411-037: Text-to-image generation involves creating images based on natural language descriptions, ranging from photorealistic scenes to semantically rich and abstract visuals~\cite{zhou2023vision+,cao2024survey .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Existing methods can be broadly categorized into three main approaches: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), diffusion models, and autoregressive approaches.
elements.sent-2411-039: GANs represent some of the earliest efforts in this field, with models such as StackGAN~\cite{stackgan and AttnGAN~\cite{attngan improving image quality through progressive generation strategies and attention mechanisms.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Despite their early successes, GANs are often hampered by issues like instability in adversarial training and mode collapse, making them less suitable for generating images of complex scenes.
elements.sent-2411-041: Diffusion models have emerged as the dominant approach, offering significant improvements in image quality by iteratively refining images through a denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-042: Notable examples include Imagen~\cite{photorealistic , which generates high-resolution images with remarkable detail.}
elements.sent-2411-043: PreciseControl~\cite{precisecontrol enable fine-grained control over specific attributes, while OpenBias~\cite{openbias incorporates mechanisms for bias detection to ensure fairness and diversity in generated outputs.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Transformer-based autoregressive models have also gained prominence due to their strong cross-modal alignment capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-045: For instance, DALL-E~\cite{ramesh2021zero employs attention mechanisms to closely map textual descriptions to visual elements.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The STAR model~\cite{star integrates multi-scale autoregressive generation with enhanced Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) to improve consistency between text and images in intricate scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Similarly, Fluid~\cite{fluid leverages multi-level attention to fine-tune the alignment of object layouts and sizes, ensuring a precise correspondence between textual inputs and visual outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-048: While these methods have made significant strides in creating natural scene imagery, they fall short when it comes to tasks that require the generation of diagrams with logical structures and explicit relationships, such as flowcharts, architecture diagrams, or other technical illustrations.
elements.sent-2411-049: Text-to-code generation involves the automatic conversion of natural language descriptions into executable code.
elements.sent-2411-050: Notable examples include Qwen2.5-Coder~\cite{qwen25 , DeepSeek-Coder-Instruct~\cite{deepseek , Code-Llama~\cite{codellama , and WizardCoder~\cite{wizardcoder , which excel at generating sophisticated code snippets that integrate architectural logic.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: In contrast, generalist text-to-code models, although not explicitly designed for code generation, have demonstrated remarkable adaptability due to their extensive pre-trainings.
elements.sent-2411-052: Open-sourced models such as Llama3~\cite{llama3 , Baichuan2~\cite{baichuan2 , InternLM2.5~\cite{internlm2 , and Qwen2~\cite{qwen2 exhibit strong capabilities across multiple programming languages, making them well-suited for a broad range of coding challenges.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Moreover, several closed-sourced models, including GPT-4o~\cite{gpt4 , Gemini~\cite{gemini , DeepSeek-2.5~\cite{deepseekv2 , and GLM-4-Plus~\cite{chatglm , offer exceptional accuracy and performance in complex code generation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Text-to-code models are also paving the way for innovations that extend beyond conventional programming tasks.
elements.sent-2411-055: The methodologies employed in text-to-code generation have inspired research into adjacent areas, such as automatic diagram generation in this work.
elements.sent-2411-056: We outline the foundational components of text-to-diagram system, detailing the processes involved in generating, coding, and editing diagrams based on user instructions.
elements.sent-2411-057: Diagram Generation Given an instruction \( x_{ins \), our system generates code \( c_{diag \), which can be compiled into a diagram \( D_{gen \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Unlike standard code generation—which focuses on executable logic—our approach emphasizes both the logical structure and the aesthetic design.
elements.sent-2411-059: Let \( f_{code (x_{ins ) \) denote the function that translates a textual instruction \( x_{ins \) into diagram-specific code \( c_{diag \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Additionally, let \( f_{img (c_{diag ) \) represent the function that compiles the diagram-specific code \( c_{diag \) into the generated diagram \( D_{gen \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The process can be formally expressed as follows: \begin{equation}D_{gen} = f_{img}(f_{code}(x_{ins})), \label{eq:diagram_gen}\end{equation} where \( D_{gen \) is the generated diagram based on the structured and logical elements specified in the user instruction.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Diagram Coding Given a diagram \( D_{gen \), our system generates structured code \( c'_{diag \) that accurately captures the logical framework and design elements of the diagram.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: This process ensures that each diagram not only conveys the intended information but also adheres to the necessary structural integrity.
elements.sent-2411-064: The process can be formulated as: \begin{equation}c'_{diag} = f_{code}^{-1}(D_{gen}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: Diagram Editing The process of modifying an existing diagram involves three main steps: diagram coding, code modification, and code-to-diagram recompilation.
elements.sent-2411-066: Initially, the original diagram \( D_{ori \) is translated back into its underlying code \( c_{ori \).}}
elements.sent-2411-067: This code is subsequently updated according to the user's editing instruction \( x_{edit \), resulting in the modified code \( c_{mod \).}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Finally, this modified code \( c_{mod \) is compiled into a new diagram \( D_{mod \).}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The editing process can be mathematically represented as: \begin{equation}D_{mod} = f_{img}(f_{mod}(f_{code}^{-1}(D_{ori}), x_{edit})), \label{eq:diagram_mod}\end{equation} where \( f_{code ^{-1 (D_{ori ) \) indicates the function that extracts the code \( c_{ori \) from the original diagram \( D_{ori \).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: The function \( f_{mod (c_{ori , x_{edit ) \) reflects the process of updating the code based on the instruction $x_{edit}$, ultimately producing the modified diagram \( D_{mod \) after recompilation via \( f_{img \).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: We summarize DiagramAgent, which comprises four core agents: Plan Agent, Code Agent, Check Agent, and Diagram-to-Code Agent in Figure~\ref{fig:workflow , highlighting the interactions between the agents and the processes involved in diagram generation, coding, and editing.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{img/img2-update3.pdf} \caption{Workflow of DiagramAgent. The DiagramAgent handles diagram generation, coding, and editing tasks, processing the user query (\ding{172}-\ding{174}) through the Plan Agent (\ding{175}), which communicates with the Code Agent (\ding{178}) for diagram generation or with the Diagram-to-Code Agent (\ding{177}) for diagram coding or editing. Code is verified and feedback provided by the Check Agent (\ding{179}).} \label{fig:workflow}
elements.sent-2411-072: The Plan Agent (\ding{175 )~\cite{autoact,qwen25 is the central component responsible for interpreting user instructions and determining the necessary steps.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The agent analyzes the instruction for completeness.
elements.sent-2411-074: If the instruction is incomplete (e.g., missing node or label information), the Plan Agent performs Query Expansion using a Large Language Model (LLM), generating a more comprehensive query \( x_{comp \), ensuring necessary elements are included.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The process can be expressed as: \begin{equation}x_{comp} = f_{expand}(x_{ins})\end{equation} Next, the complete query \( x_{comp \) is passed to the Code Agent, which generates the corresponding code \( c_{diag \).}}
elements.sent-2411-076: For diagram coding, the Plan Agent handles the initial diagram \(D_{ori}\) by invoking the Diagram-to-Code Agent \( f_{code ^{-1 (D_{ori ) \) (\ding{177 ), which translates the diagram into its code form.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: For diagram editing, the Plan Agent receives the editing query \( x_{edit \) along with the diagram \( D_{ori \).}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Based on the user's request for changes, the Plan Agent again calls the Diagram-to-Code Agent \( f_{code ^{-1 (D_{ori ) \) (\ding{177 ) to convert the provided diagram into code.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Then, the Plan Agent sends both the code and the editing query \( x_{edit \) to the Code Agent (\ding{178 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The Code Agent (\ding{178 )~\cite{unifying,qwen25 is responsible for transforming the processed user instructions from the Plan Agent (\ding{175 ) into executable code.}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: For diagram generation, the Code Agent takes the complete query \( x_{comp \) and generates diagram-specific code \( c_{diag \) as follows: \begin{equation}c_{diag} = f_{code}(x_{comp})\end{equation} For diagram editing, the Code Agent takes the original code \( c_{ori \) and applies the modifications according to the query \( x_{edit \) to generate modified code \( c_{mod \).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The code is passed to the Check Agent (\ding{179 ) for debugging and refinement, ensuring that the code is both syntactically and logically correct.}
elements.sent-2411-083: We optimize the Code Agent by training it to generate code \( c \) that closely matches a reference code \( c_{ref \).}
elements.sent-2411-084: This can be formalized as minimizing the following objective: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \min_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{(x, c_{ref})} \left[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{code}}(f_{code_\theta}(x), c_{ref}) \right],\end{equation} where \( \theta \) represents the parameters of the model, and \( \mathcal{L _{\text{code \) is the loss function, which measures the discrepancy between the generated code and the reference code.}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: The Diagram-to-Code Agent (\ding{177 ) is responsible for converting a given diagram \( D_{ori \) into its corresponding code \( c'_{diag \), facilitating the diagram coding task.}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: The Diagram-to-Code Agent captures the diagram's logical structure and visual elements, translating them into structured code.
elements.sent-2411-087: This transformation process is formally expressed as: \begin{equation}c'_{diag} = f_{\text{diagram-to-code}}(D_{ori})\end{equation} where \( c'_{diag \) denotes the generated code based on the elements of the diagram $D_{ori}$.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The Diagram-to-Code Agent then sends the generated $c'_{diag}$ to the Check Agent (\ding{176 ) for validation.}
elements.sent-2411-089: If necessary, feedback is provided to refine \( c'_{diag \).}
elements.sent-2411-090: The objective is to generate code \( c'_{diag \) that closely matches a reference code \( c_{ref \), ensuring that the code preserves the diagram's logical and visual integrity.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: This optimization is formalized as minimizing the following loss function: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \min_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{(D_{ori}, c_{ref})} \left[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{code}}(f_{\text{diagram-to-code}_\theta}(D_{ori}), c_{ref}) \right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-092: The Check Agent (\ding{179 )~\cite{qwen25 is responsible for verifying the correctness and completeness of the code generated by the Code Agent (\ding{178 ) and the Diagram-to-Code Agent(\ding{177 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Its role involves two critical stages: debugging and verification.
elements.sent-2411-094: First, the Check Agent compiles the generated code.
elements.sent-2411-095: If errors are detected, they are returned to the Code Agent or Diagram-to-Code Agent for correction and regeneration.
elements.sent-2411-096: Once the code compiles successfully, the Check Agent uses GPT-4o~\cite{gpt4 to verify the completeness of the code, ensuring that all necessary elements are included.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The process can be summarized as follows: { \begin{equation}f_{\text{check}}(c) = f_{\text{debug}}(c_{diag}, c_{mod}, c'_{diag}) + f_{\text{verify}}(c_{diag}, c_{mod}, c'_{diag})\end{equation} where \( f_{\text{debug (c) \) returns errors if present, and \( f_{\text{verify (c) \) evaluates the completeness of the diagram.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}f_{check}(c) = f_{debug}(c_{diag}, c_{mod}, c'_{diag}) + f_{verify}(c_{diag}, c_{mod}, c'_{diag})\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-098: We introduce DiagramGenBenchmark, which focuses on transforming textual descriptions into structured diagram representations.
elements.sent-2411-099: It includes various types of diagrams such as model architecture diagrams, flowcharts, line charts, directed and undirected graphs, tables, bar charts, and mind maps.
elements.sent-2411-100: The data is sourced from HuggingFace's VGQA~\cite{vgbench , datikz~\cite{AutomaTikZ , and datikz-v2~\cite{AutomaTikZ datasets, as well as open-source repositories on GitHub and Overleaf, which are licensed under CC BY 4.0 or MIT.}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: These repositories predominantly feature diagram code written in LaTeX or DOT languages.
elements.sent-2411-102: Due to the limited space, we provide the benchmark dataset details in the Appendix~\ref{app:data_details .}
elements.sent-2411-103: Figure~\ref{fig:data_examples provides example queries and diagrams, inlcuding model architecture, flowcharts, line charts, directed/undirected graphs, tables, bar charts, and mind maps.}
elements.sent-2411-104: As detailed in Table~\ref{tab:data_statistics , the diagram generation/coding task contains 6713 training samples and 270 testing samples, while the diagram editing task includes 1400 training samples and 200 testing samples.}
elements.sent-2411-105: The query lengths and code lengths vary significantly, reflecting the complexity of the tasks.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Key statistics of DiagramGenBenchmark.} \label{tab:data_statistics} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{0.6mm}{ \begin{tabular}{lcc} Statistic Generation / Coding Editing Total Samples 6983 1600 Training Samples 6713 1400 Testing Samples 270 200 Diagram Type \quad Model Architecture 3000 (42.96\%) 706 (44.13\%) \quad Flowchart 1500 (21.48\%) 320 (20\%) \quad Line Chart 1142 (16.35\%) 227 (14.19\%) \quad Directed Graph 787 (11.27\%) 217 (13.56\%) \quad Table 114 (1.63\%) 19 (1.19\%) \quad Bar Chart 227 (3.25\%) 77 (4.81\%) \quad Mind Map 140 (2.00\%) 16 (1\%) \quad Undirected Graph 73 (1.05\%) 18 (1.12\%) Query Length (tokens) \quad Minimum 18 40 \quad Maximum 561 499 \quad Average 247 133 Complete Query Length (tokens) \quad Minimum 404 115 \quad Maximum 4015 3419 \quad Average 1900 98 Code Length (tokens) \quad Minimum 60 116 \quad Maximum 75612 4727 \quad Average 1701 1009.6 \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Code Generation Metrics: For the diagram generation and editing tasks, we evaluate the generated code using: \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Pass@1 (↑): Measures accuracy of generated code.
elements.sent-2411-108: ROUGE-L (↑): Measures similarity with reference code based on longest common subsequence.
elements.sent-2411-109: CodeBLEU (↑): Assess semantic similarity with reference code.
elements.sent-2411-110: Edit Distance (↓): Computes the edit operations required to match the reference.
elements.sent-2411-112: PSNR (↑), MS-SSIM (↑): Structural and clarity metrics.
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Ablation study for diagram generation task (Code Agent). Each result shows the performance of DiagramAgent Code Agent under various component configurations, with the decrease in each metric from the full model indicated in parentheses.} \label{tab:diagram generation-ablation} \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c} Diagram generation Size Pass@1↑ ROUGE-L↑ CodeBLEU↑ Edit Dist.↓ chrF↑ RUBY↑ CLIP-FID↓ LPIPS↓ PSNR↑ MS-SSIM↑ DiagramAgent 7B 58.15 51.97 86.83 74.62 53.49 39.71 11.16 45.95 6.38 24.78 -- w/o GPT-4o 7B 57.78 51.95 86.35 74.71 52.81 39.33 11.23 46.66 6.33 20.80 (-0.37) (-0.02) (-0.48) (+0.09) (-0.68) (-0.38) (+0.07) (+0.71) (-0.05) (-3.98) -- w/o Compiler 7B 57.41 51.94 85.78 74.68 51.74 39.65 11.29 48.13 5.93 24.10 (-0.74) (-0.03) (-1.05) (+0.06) (-1.75) (-0.06) (+0.13) (+2.18) (-0.45) (-0.68) -- w/o GPT-4o \& Compiler 7B 57.41 51.87 85.74 74.79 51.69 38.77 11.30 48.17 5.85 20.37 (-0.74) (-0.10) (-1.09) (+0.17) (-1.80) (-0.94) (+0.14) (+2.22) (-0.53) (-4.41) \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-113: DiagramAgent performs diagram generation and editing through four core agents: Plan Agent, Code Agent, Check Agent, and Diagram-to-Code Agent.
elements.sent-2411-114: The Plan Agent utilizes the Qwen-72B~\cite{qwen25 to effectively distinguish and interpret the user's query tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-115: The Code Agent is based on Qwen2.5-Coder-7B~\cite{qwen25 , fine-tuned for four epochs with a maximum input length of 8192 tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Diagram-to-Code Agent uses Qwen2-VL-7B~\cite{qwen2-vl in its default configuration.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Experiments are conducted on an 8$\times$80G A100 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-118: We evaluate DiagramAgent's Code Agent against 16 mainstream models.
elements.sent-2411-119: The specialized code models include Qwen2.5-Coder~\cite{qwen25 , DeepSeek-Coder-Instruct~\cite{deepseek , Code-Llama~\cite{codellama , WizardCoder~\cite{wizardcoder , Codegeex4-all~\cite{zheng2023codegeex , Starcoder2~\cite{li2023starcoder , and Yi-Coder~\cite{yicoder .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Open-source models feature Llama-3.1~\cite{llama3 , Baichuan2~\cite{baichuan2 , Internlm2\_5~\cite{internlm2 , Yi-1.5~\cite{young2024yi , and Qwen2~\cite{qwen25 , while close-source models include GPT-4o~\cite{gpt4 , DeepSeek V2.5~\cite{deepseekv2 , GLM-4-plus~\cite{chatglm , and Gemini~\cite{gemini .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: Similarly, we compare the Diagram-to-Code Agent's performance on the diagram coding against open-source models include Yi-VL~\cite{yivl , Qwen2-VL~\cite{qwen2-vl , Internlm-Xcomposer2.5~\cite{zhang2024internlm , Llama-3.2-Vision~\cite{llama3 , Phi-3.5-vision~\cite{phi3 , Llava-v1.6~\cite{liu2024llavanext , Cogvlm2-llama3~\cite{hong2024cogvlm2 , and Deepseek-vl~\cite{deepseekvl .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Close-source models include GPT-4o~\cite{gpt4 , GLM-4-plus~\cite{chatglm , and Gemini-1.5-pro~\cite{gemini .}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: DiagramAgent's Code Agent demonstrates outstanding performance on the diagram generation task, achieving top scores across both code accuracy and image fidelity metrics, as shown in Table \ref{tab:diagram generation-main .}
elements.sent-2411-124: In terms of code quality, DiagramAgent achieves leading results with metrics such as Pass@1 (58.15), ROUGE-L (51.97), and CodeBLEU (86.83), among others, highlighting its capability to generate accurate and robust code representations.
elements.sent-2411-125: These results demonstrate the effectiveness of DiagramAgent in generating structured, accurate, and high-quality diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-126: For image quality, it also excels with PSNR (6.38), LPIPS (45.95), and others, confirming its ability to maintain high visual fidelity in generated diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-127: The ablation study in Table \ref{tab:diagram generation-ablation demonstrates the critical role of the Check Agent in enhancing DiagramAgent's performance.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Removing the verification module (GPT-4o) leads to the chrF decrease of -0.68 and the LPIPS increase of +0.71.
elements.sent-2411-129: Excluding the debugging component (compiler) results in a significant chrF drop of -1.75 and an LPIPS increase of +2.18, impacting both code quality and image fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-130: Together, the debugging and verification achieve the highest scores across metrics, underscoring their combined value in producing precise diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-131: Overall, this ablation study demonstrates that both debugging and verification are essential in diagram generation.
elements.sent-2411-132: DiagramAgent's model, configured with compiler-based debugging followed by GPT-4o verification, achieves the highest performance across several metrics in the diagram coding task, as illustrated in Table \ref{tab:image-to-code-main .}
elements.sent-2411-133: Key metrics include Pass@1 (68.89), ROUGE-L (48.99), codeBLEU (84.64), demonstrating DiagramAgent's effectiveness in generating high-quality code from images.
elements.sent-2411-134: Compared to both open-source models like Qwen2-VL-7B-Instruct and closed-source models such as gpt-4o, DiagramAgent consistently excels, highlighting its robustness in tasks requiring precise visual-to-code translation.

The best result in each metric is bolded.} \label{tab:image-to-code-main} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{0.7mm}{ \begin{tabular}{l|c|c|c|c} Model Size Pass@1↑ ROUGE-L↑ codeBLEU↑ Yi-VL~\cite{yivl} 34B 2.22 20.01 70.57 Qwen2-VL~\cite{qwen2-vl} 7B 28.89 31.74 80.04 Internlm-xcomposer2.5~\cite{zhang2024internlm} 7B 3.33 28.47 77.35 Llama-3.2-Vision~\cite{llama3} 11B 27.78 21.94 75.37 Phi-3.5-vision~\cite{phi3} 4B 24.07 27.53 76.56 Llava-v1.6~\cite{liu2024llavanext} 34B 8.89 26.68 76.53 Cogvlm2-llama3~\cite{hong2024cogvlm2} 19B 3.70 14.42 70.72 Deepseek-vl~\cite{deepseekvl} 7B 50.74 25.18 76.48 GPT-4o~\cite{gpt4} - 64.07 39.95 81.78 GLM-4-plus~\cite{chatglm} - 51.48 35.92 80.16 Gemini-1.5-pro~\cite{gemini} - 17.78 38.66 80.75 DiagramAgent 7B 68.89 48.99 84.64 \end{tabular}} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\columnwidth} \caption{Ablation study for diagram coding task (Diagram-to-Code Agent). elements.sent-2411-137: Excluding the compiler module reduces Pass@1 by -15.56 and ROUGE-L by -0.53.
elements.sent-2411-138: The full model configuration, which includes both debugging and verification, achieves optimal results across all metrics, affirming the essential role of these components in enhancing diagram coding performance.

elements.sent-2411-140: DiagramAgent’s Code Agent achieves superior results on the diagram editing task, demonstrating exceptional performance in both code accuracy and image quality metrics, as shown in Table \ref{tab:modify-diagram generation-main .}
elements.sent-2411-141: In terms of code generation, DiagramAgent leads with top scores in Pass@1 (98.00), ROUGE-L (98.41), and CodeBLEU (99.93), underscoring its capability for precise and reliable code outputs.
elements.sent-2411-142: For image quality, DiagramAgent also excels, achieving outstanding results in CLIP-FID (1.08), LPIPS (40.64), and MS-SSIM (97.00), although its PSNR (13.18) is slightly lower than WizardCoder, potentially due to DiagramAgent's focus on overall image fidelity rather than absolute sharpness.
elements.sent-2411-143: DiagramAgent consistently outperforms baseline models across comprehensive metrics, validating its strong adaptability and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-144: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:modify-diagram generation-ablation , removing the verification module results in a Pass@1 decrease of -1.00 and a chrF reduction of -0.08, highlighting its role in ensuring code accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Excluding the debugging module notably impacts CodeBLEU by -0.74 and increases LPIPS by +1.53, affecting both code quality and visual fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-146: Interestingly, while removing the debugging module led to a decrease in overall fidelity, PSNR showed a slight improvement, possibly due to the model's focus shifting towards simpler representations, which, though less precise, may result in visually sharper images.
elements.sent-2411-147: Removing both components leads to larger performance declines, including a Pass@1 reduction of -2.50 and an MS-SSIM decrease of -3.07, confirming the necessity of debugging and verification for maintaining both high accuracy and fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-148: To assess DiagramAgent’s performance, three raters with master’s degrees evaluated two core tasks—the diagram generation task and the diagram editing task—using a 1-5 scale.
elements.sent-2411-149: A score of 1 represents minimal satisfaction in terms of code accuracy and diagram fidelity, while a score of 5 indicates the highest satisfaction.
elements.sent-2411-150: The evaluation criteria focused on the similarity between the generated diagram and the reference diagram.
elements.sent-2411-151: Figure~\ref{fig:human-eval presents the scores across models.}
elements.sent-2411-152: DiagramAgent achieves state-of-the-art scores in both tasks, demonstrating superior accuracy compared to other models.
elements.sent-2411-153: Notably, the results show that DiagramAgent performs better on the diagram editing task than on the diagram generation task.
elements.sent-2411-154: This difference suggests that diagram editing task, which involves modifying an existing codebase, is comparatively easier due to the established structure, whereas the diagram generation task requires generating a diagram from scratch, posing a higher challenge given the dependency on instruction quality.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{img/img6.pdf} \caption{Human evaluation results for different models on diagram generation and Modify diagram generation tasks.} \label{fig:human-eval}
elements.sent-2411-155: Consequently, achieving accurate diagrams in a single generation step remains a significant challenge, often requiring multiple iterations to refine the output.
elements.sent-2411-156: This observation highlights the importance of DiagramAgent's capability for iterative diagram editing, enabling the generation of precise and refined diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-157: We also provide detailed error analysis in Appendix~\ref{app:error-analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-158: In this work, we present DiagramAgent, a framework for Text-to-Diagram generation, alongside DiagramGenBenchmark, a comprehensive dataset that encompasses eight distinct types of structured diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-159: Our extensive experimental evaluations, which include both objective metrics and human assessments, demonstrate that DiagramAgent significantly outperforms existing open-source and close-source models in diagram generation, coding, and editing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-160: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first framework specifically designed not only for generating structured diagrams from textual descriptions but also for editing them.
elements.sent-2411-161: This work sets a new benchmark for text-to-diagram tasks, contributing significantly to the fields of structured diagram generation and editing.
elements.sent-2411-162: However, there are still areas for further refinement.
elements.sent-2411-163: DiagramAgent's handling of more complex structures, particularly with regard to intricate spatial relationships, remains an area for future development.
elements.sent-2411-164: Additionally, while diagram editing has reached a high level of accuracy, challenges persist in diagram generation, where achieving consistent and precise coding remains critical for producing reliable and accurate modifications.
elements.sent-2411-165: In constructing DiagramGenBenchmark, we employed a rigorous data curation process to ensure quality and diversity across the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-166: This section details our multi-step pipeline, from raw data collection to task-specific dataset creation, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:data_curation .}
elements.sent-2411-167: We gathered raw Tex and DOT language-based code data from multiple sources, including the VGQA~\cite{vgbench , datikz~\cite{AutomaTikZ , and datikz-v2~\cite{AutomaTikZ datasets on Hugging Face, as well as publicly available repositories on GitHub and Overleaf.}}}
elements.sent-2411-168: These sources yielded a comprehensive collection of over 13,000 raw code samples, originally sourced from various research papers on arXiv.
elements.sent-2411-169: Collected raw code samples were subjected to manual filtering.
elements.sent-2411-170: Out of 13,000 initial samples, we retained only those that could be compiled successfully into images, ensuring code-to-diagram consistency.
elements.sent-2411-171: We executed each code sample using respective Tex and DOT compilers and stored each code-image pair in separate directories.
elements.sent-2411-172: After this filtering, we obtained 6,983 code-image pairs as the final dataset.
elements.sent-2411-173: To label the data, we defined eight diagram categories: model architecture diagram, flowchart, line chart, directed graph, undirected graph, table, bar chart, and mind map.
elements.sent-2411-174: Automatic labeling was conducted using the GPT-4o~\cite{gpt4 .}
elements.sent-2411-175: Dataset Tasks: We constructed three distinct tasks to enhance the benchmark's applicability:.
elements.sent-2411-176: Diagram generation: This task involves generating code from human instructions.
elements.sent-2411-177: We created human-like instructions by reverse-engineering existing open-source diagram code using the closed-source GPT-4o model.
elements.sent-2411-178: A total of 6,713 training instances and 270 testing instances were generated for this task.
elements.sent-2411-179: Diagram coding: This task requires generating compliable diagram code from an image.
elements.sent-2411-180: Using the code and image pairs from the diagram generation task, we retained only image-code pairs and created a set of 6,713 training instances and 270 testing instances.
elements.sent-2411-181: Diagram editing: This task simulates code editing based on revision instructions.
elements.sent-2411-182: We generated modification suggestions using GPT-4o and applied them to the original code with the CodeGeeX-4~\cite{zheng2023codegeex model.}
elements.sent-2411-183: Only compilable, unique modified codes were selected, resulting in 1,400 training instances and 200 testing instances.
elements.sent-2411-184: Figure~\ref{fig:data_distribution illustrates the data distribution for different diagram types in the diagram generation / diagram coding task and the diagram editing task.}
elements.sent-2411-185: This appendix provides a comprehensive view of the prompt designs used in DiagramAgent's core components, including the Plan Agent, Complete Query handling, Diagram-to-Code Agent, Code Agent, and Check Agent.
elements.sent-2411-186: These prompts support different tasks such as drawing, modifying diagrams, and error checking, ensuring that each module contributes to accurate and coherent diagram generation and modification.

elements.figure-2411-020: \caption{Comparison of DiagramGenBenchmark with Existing Benchmarks. "NL" indicates Natural Language inputs, "I" represents Image inputs, and "Code" denotes code output. Evaluation metrics vary across benchmarks, including pass rate, multi-level metrics, and other task-specific measures.} \label{tab:comparison} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{0.4mm}{ \begin{tabular}{lcccccc} Benchmark Source \# of Chart Types \# of Test Instances Input Format Output Format Evaluation Metric HumanEval~\cite{chen2021evaluating} Human Curated - 164 Code Code Pass Rate MBPP~\cite{austin2021program} Human Curated - 500 NL+Code Code Pass Rate MMCode~\cite{li2024mmcode} Crawl - 263 NL+Code Code Pass Rate MatPlotBench~\cite{yang2024matplotagent} Human Curated 13 100 NL Code GPT-4V Score Design2Code~\cite{si2024design2code} Crawl - 484 I+NL Code Multi-Level Plot2Code~\cite{wu2024plot2code} Human Curated 6 132 I Code Pass Rate, Text-Match, etc. Diagram generation (Ours) Human Curated 8 270 NL Code,Diagram Pass Rate, CodeBLEU, ROUGE-L, etc. Diagram coding (Ours) Human Curated 8 270 I Code Pass Rate, CodeBLEU, ROUGE-L, etc. Diagram eidting (Ours) Human Curated 8 200 NL+Code Code,Diagram Pass Rate, CodeBLEU, ROUGE-L, etc. \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-187: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:comparison , DiagramGenBenchmark differs from existing benchmarks by providing both natural language and image inputs specifically for structured diagram generation and editing.}
elements.sent-2411-188: Unlike traditional code generation benchmarks focused on simple text-based tasks, our benchmark encompasses a diverse range of diagram types and incorporates multi-level evaluation metrics (e.g., Pass@1, CodeBLEU, CLIP-FID, PSNR).
elements.sent-2411-189: Additionally, DiagramGenBenchmark includes three specialized tasks—diagram generation, diagram coding, and diagram editing—designed to evaluate both diagram creation and editing capabilities, making it a comprehensive resource for advancing multimodal diagram generation research.
elements.sent-2411-190: To evaluate the performance of DiagramAgent, three evaluators with master’s degrees independently rated the quality of generated diagrams on a scale from 1 to 5.
elements.sent-2411-191: The evaluation focused on the similarity between the generated diagrams and reference diagrams for two core tasks: the diagram generation and the diagram editing.
elements.sent-2411-192: Each score represents the following specific criteria:.
elements.sent-2411-193: Each evaluator independently scored each of the $N$ test items, resulting in three scores per item.
elements.sent-2411-194: To calculate the mean score for each item, we average the three scores from the evaluators: \begin{equation}\text{Mean Score for Item } i = \frac{S^{(i)}_{1} + S^{(i)}_{2} + S^{(i)}_{3}}{3} \label{eq:item_mean_score}\end{equation} where $S^{(i) _{1}$, $S^{(i) _{2}$, and $S^{(i) _{3}$ represent the scores given by the three evaluators for item $i$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}Mean Score for Item  i = \frac{S^{(i)}_{1} + S^{(i)}_{2} + S^{(i)}_{3}}{3} \label{eq:item_mean_score}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-195: After obtaining the mean score for each item, we calculate the overall average score for the model across all $N$ test items.
elements.sent-2411-196: This final score is computed as: \begin{equation}\text{Final Score for Model} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Mean Score for Item } i}{N} \label{eq:model_final_score}\end{equation} This final score provides a standardized measure of the model’s ability to generate diagrams that closely resemble the reference diagrams.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}Final Score for Model = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} Mean Score for Item  i}{N} \label{eq:model_final_score}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-197: It reflects the model's performance across each task.
elements.sent-2411-198: This appendix provides a complete analysis of the experimental results for the diagram coding task with DiagramAgent, expanding on the brief introduction provided in Section \cref{Diagram_Coding of the main text.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Due to space constraints, only key findings were highlighted in the main text, while this appendix presents a more in-depth evaluation of DiagramAgent’s performance relative to other models across various metrics, as shown in Tables \ref{tab:image-to-code-main-complete and \ref{tab:image-to-code-ablation-complete .}}
elements.figure-2411-021: \caption{Main results for diagram coding task (Diagram-to-Code Agent). The best result in each metric is bolded.} \label{tab:image-to-code-main-complete} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{4.2mm}{ \begin{tabular}{l|c|c|c|c|c|c|c} Model Size Pass@1↑ ROUGE-L↑ codeBLEU↑ Edit Dist.↓ chrF↑ RUBY↑ Yi-VL~\cite{yivl} 34B 2.22 20.01 70.57 95.43 11.68 12.53 Qwen2-VL~\cite{qwen2-vl} 8B 28.89 31.74 80.04 88.13 28.39 21.21 Internlm-xcomposer2.5~\cite{zhang2024internlm} 7B 3.33 28.47 77.35 92.35 18.74 17.97 Llama-3.2-Vision~\cite{llama3} 11B 27.78 21.94 75.37 92.92 16.37 13.95 Phi-3.5-vision~\cite{phi3} 4B 24.07 27.53 76.56 90.01 20.86 17.96 Llava-v1.6~\cite{liu2024llavanext} 34B 8.89 26.68 76.53 93.46 21.00 16.30 Cogvlm2-llama3~\cite{hong2024cogvlm2} 19B 3.70 14.42 70.72 97.07 8.27 8.91 Deepseek-vl~\cite{deepseekvl} 7B 50.74 25.18 76.48 88.82 18.35 16.13 GPT-4o~\cite{gpt4} - 64.07 39.95 81.78 86.68 34.40 26.18 GLM-4-plus~\cite{chatglm} - 51.48 35.92 80.16 86.12 29.10 24.60 Gemini-1.5-pro~\cite{gemini} - 17.78 38.66 80.75 88.05 30.00 25.62 DiagramAgent 7B 68.89 48.99 84.64 72.74 46.98 37.46 \end{tabular}}
elements.figure-2411-022: \caption{Ablation study for diagram coding task (Diagram-to-Code Agent). Each result shows the performance of DiagramAgent under various component configurations, with the decrease in each metric from the full model indicated in parentheses.} \label{tab:image-to-code-ablation-complete} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{4.2mm}{ \begin{tabular}{l|c|c|c|c|c|c|c} Diagram Coding Size Pass@1↑ ROUGE-L↑ codeBLEU↑ Edit Dist.↓ chrF↑ RUBY↑ DiagramAgent 7B 68.89 48.99 84.64 72.74 46.98 37.46 -- w/o GPT-4o 7B 62.59 48.71 84.57 72.69 46.49 37.22 (-6.30) (-0.28) (-0.07) (-0.05) (-0.49) (-0.24) -- w/o Compiler 7B 53.33 48.46 84.52 73.51 46.91 36.66 (-15.56) (-0.53) (-0.12) (+0.77) (-0.07) (-0.80) -- w/o GPT-4o \& Compiler 7B 52.59 47.81 84.21 74.56 45.28 35.81 (-16.30) (-1.18) (-0.43) (+1.82) (-1.70) (-1.65) \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-200: Table \ref{tab:image-to-code-main-complete summarizes the main results for the diagram coding task, where DiagramAgent consistently outperforms other models across all key evaluation metrics, demonstrating its robustness and precision in translating diagrams into code.}
elements.sent-2411-201: For Pass@1, DiagramAgent achieves 68.89, which is significantly higher than the scores of open-source models, such as Qwen2-VL (28.89) and Llama-3.2-Vision (27.78), as well as the scores of closed-source models such as GPT-4o (64.07) and GLM-4-plus (51.48).
elements.sent-2411-202: This high Pass@1 indicates that DiagramAgent performs well in generating correct code on the first attempt, reducing the need for iterative refinements and thus enhancing efficiency in the coding process.
elements.sent-2411-203: The ROUGE-L score of 48.99 for DiagramAgent highlights its strength in preserving the structural and sequential accuracy of generated code, outperforming models such as GLM-4-plus (35.92) and GPT-4o (39.95).
elements.sent-2411-204: ROUGE-L is crucial in tasks that require alignment with the target’s structural elements, and DiagramAgent’s high score reflects its ability to capture complex syntactic patterns essential for correct code generation from diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-205: Additionally, DiagramAgent attains a codeBLEU score of 84.64, the highest among all models, which captures both syntactic and semantic alignment with the target code.
elements.sent-2411-206: Compared to other models, such as Qwen2-VL (80.04) and Gemini-1.5-pro (80.75), DiagramAgent demonstrates a marked improvement in generating both structurally and functionally accurate code, underscoring its capability to handle the nuanced requirements of diagram coding tasks.
elements.sent-2411-207: In terms of Edit Distance, DiagramAgent achieves a score of 72.74, which is the lowest among all models, indicating that fewer modifications are needed to align generated code with the target.
elements.sent-2411-208: Lower Edit Distance highlights DiagramAgent’s initial output accuracy, minimizing post-generation adjustments and improving overall workflow efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-209: This metric contrasts with higher Edit Distances observed in other models, such as Cogvlm2-llama3 (97.07) and Yi-VL (95.43), which indicate a greater need for corrections.
elements.sent-2411-210: Additionally, DiagramAgent’s chrF score of 46.98, higher than all compared models, reflects the fine-grained character-level alignment achieved by DiagramAgent, essential for high-quality code generation.
elements.sent-2411-211: Finally, DiagramAgent’s RUBY score of 37.46 is the highest across models, underscoring the model’s robustness and its capacity to generate code that aligns well with human coding standards, surpassing both close-source models, such as GPT-4o (26.18) and open-source options like Qwen2-VL (21.21).
elements.sent-2411-212: These comprehensive results collectively affirm DiagramAgent’s superior performance in the diagram coding task across all evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-213: Table \ref{tab:image-to-code-ablation-complete presents the ablation study results, which evaluate the contributions of the GPT-4o verification and compiler debugging modules to DiagramAgent’s overall performance.}
elements.sent-2411-214: When the GPT-4o verification module is removed, DiagramAgent’s Pass@1 score drops by 6.30, while ROUGE-L decreases slightly by 0.28.
elements.sent-2411-215: These reductions suggest that the verification module enhances first-attempt accuracy, as indicated by the drop in Pass@1, and plays a role in preserving the structural fidelity of generated code, reflected in the ROUGE-L score.
elements.sent-2411-216: The minor reduction in codeBLEU by 0.07 further points to the verification component’s contribution to maintaining both syntactic and semantic consistency, while the minimal changes in Edit Distance (+0.05) and chrF (-0.49) indicate that this module has a moderate impact on fine-grained accuracy and error reduction.
elements.sent-2411-217: Removing the compiler debugging module has a more substantial effect, leading to a 15.56 decrease in Pass@1 and a 0.53 drop in ROUGE-L.
elements.sent-2411-218: These changes reflect the compiler’s crucial role in achieving syntactic accuracy, as it likely identifies and corrects structural inconsistencies early in the generation process.
elements.sent-2411-219: The Edit Distance increase of +0.77 suggests that the absence of debugging requires more extensive post-generation edits to achieve target alignment.
elements.sent-2411-220: The drops in chrF (-0.07) and RUBY (-0.80) underscore the compiler’s importance in ensuring character-level precision and maintaining the overall quality of the generated code.
elements.sent-2411-221: When both the GPT-4o verification and compiler debugging components are removed, DiagramAgent’s performance declines across all metrics, with a Pass@1 decrease of 16.30 and an Edit Distance increase of +1.82.
elements.sent-2411-222: The additional decreases in ROUGE-L (-1.18), codeBLEU (-0.43), chrF (-1.70), and RUBY (-1.65) further underscore the importance of these components for achieving DiagramAgent’s high standards in code generation.
elements.sent-2411-223: Without these modules, the model’s outputs show a higher propensity for errors, requiring greater manual adjustment and impacting both structural and semantic accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-224: This configuration demonstrates the complementary roles of verification and debugging in maintaining DiagramAgent’s precision and robustness in diagram coding tasks.
elements.sent-2411-225: Our analysis of errors covers three primary tasks: diagram generation, diagram editing, and diagram coding, with common error types illustrated in Figures \ref{fig:instruction-to-code-errors , \ref{fig:diagram-to-code-errors , and \ref{fig:code-modification-errors .}}}
elements.sent-2411-226: Identifying and addressing these errors is crucial for enhancing the overall accuracy of generated diagrams, and it represents an essential focus for future work.
elements.sent-2411-227: For the diagram generation, three prominent error types emerge:.
elements.sent-2411-228: Diagram Shape Understanding Error: This error occurs when the model misinterprets the shape requirements specified in the user query.
elements.sent-2411-229: For instance, the model may generate incorrect or overly simplified shapes, as seen in Figure \ref{fig:instruction-to-code-errors (left).}
elements.sent-2411-230: This typically arises from ambiguity in the instruction or ineffective shape recognition capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-231: Diagram Structure Understanding Error: The model occasionally fails to capture the hierarchical or relational structure of diagrams, leading to incorrect element placements or connections, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:instruction-to-code-errors (center).}
elements.sent-2411-232: This error is often due to limited structural parsing of complex diagram elements.
elements.sent-2411-233: Diagram Content Understanding Error: Errors in content understanding arise when the model misinterprets content-specific details, such as labels or numeric data, leading to inaccuracies in representation, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:instruction-to-code-errors (right).}
elements.sent-2411-234: This may result from insufficient parsing of content semantics in instructions.
elements.figure-2411-023: \begin{minipage}{\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{img_appendix/img8.pdf} \caption{Common Error Types in the Diagram Generation: (Left) Shape Understanding Error, (Center) Structure Understanding Error, (Right) Content Understanding Error} \label{fig:instruction-to-code-errors} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{img_appendix/img10.pdf} \caption{Common Error Types in the Diagram Coding: (Left) Position Understanding Error, (Center) Layout Understanding Error, (Right) Color Understanding Error} \label{fig:diagram-to-code-errors} \end{minipage}
elements.figure-2411-024: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{img_appendix/img9.pdf} \caption{Common Error Types in the Diagram Editing: (Left) Color Understanding Error, (Center) Line Type Understanding Error, (Right) Font Type Understanding Error} \label{fig:code-modification-errors}
elements.sent-2411-235: For the diagram coding, errors typically involve spatial and layout issues:.
elements.sent-2411-236: Position Understanding Error: Misinterpretations of element positions result in misplaced components, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:diagram-to-code-errors (left).}
elements.sent-2411-237: This error often stems from insufficient spatial understanding of diagram elements.
elements.sent-2411-238: Layout Understanding Error: Incorrect layout arrangements, where elements are poorly aligned or spaced, disrupt diagram coherence, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:diagram-to-code-errors (center).}
elements.sent-2411-239: This error arises from limitations in layout parsing and spatial arrangement.
elements.sent-2411-240: Color Understanding Error: Similar to the diagram editing, color errors also appear in diagram coding, leading to misinterpreted color schemes that impact semantic meaning, as seen in Figure \ref{fig:diagram-to-code-errors (right).}
elements.sent-2411-241: This is due to inadequate interpretation of color attributes in diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-242: In the diagram editing, the following errors are most prevalent:.
elements.sent-2411-243: Color Understanding Error: Color misinterpretation often leads to incorrect assignments, reducing visual clarity, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:code-modification-errors (left).}
elements.sent-2411-244: This issue typically arises from inadequate parsing of color-related instructions.
elements.sent-2411-245: Line Type Understanding Error: Misunderstandings in line type specifications, such as solid vs. dashed lines, lead to errors in edge representation, as seen in Figure \ref{fig:code-modification-errors (center).}
elements.sent-2411-246: This stems from insufficient differentiation between line types in code interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-247: Font Type Understanding Error: Incorrect font usage in diagrams, especially for labels, affects readability and visual coherence, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:code-modification-errors (right).}
elements.sent-2411-248: This issue is often due to limitations in font parsing and application.
elements.sent-2411-249: Addressing these errors is essential for enhancing the precision and fidelity of the generated diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-250: Future work should focus on refining the model’s capabilities in shape, structure, content, color, line type, font, position, and layout understanding to better align generated diagrams with user expectations.
elements.sent-2411-251: After receiving paper reviews, authors may optionally submit a rebuttal to address the reviewers' comments, which will be limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-252: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-253: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-254: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-255: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-256: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-257: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-258: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
elements.sent-2411-259: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-260: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-261: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-262: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-263: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-264: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-265: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-266: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-267: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-268: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-269: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-271: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-272: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-273: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-274: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-275: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-276: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in \cref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-277: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-278: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-279: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.
elements.figure-2411-025: \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{0.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Example of caption. It is set in Roman so that mathematics (always set in Roman: $B \sin A = A \sin B$) may be included without an ugly clash.} \label{fig:onecol}
elements.sent-2411-280: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about \cref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-283: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-284: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-285: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-286: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
elements.sent-2411-287: When placing figures in \LaTeX, it is almost always best to use \verb+\includegraphics+, and to specify the figure width as a multiple of the line width as in the example below { .}
